critic_raw:
Issue 1: Seasonal case-mix difference between comparison periods.
Issue 2: Three concurrent interventions — model, antibiotic protocol, surge unit — cannot be separated.
Issue 3: Model may influence lactate ordering, which defines the analysis cohort.
defender_raw: Round 1: On Issue 1 — concede. Same-season prior-year comparison is the minimum needed to control for seasonal effects. On Issue 2 — concede. Three simultaneous changes make causal attribution impossible without a concurrent control group. The antibiotic protocol is particularly important — it directly affects sepsis mortality. On Issue 3 — concede. If the model influenced lactate ordering, the cohort composition changed and the comparison is not controlled for this selection.

debate_rounds.critic: Full concession. Critique wins. The mortality comparison cannot be attributed to the model. A concurrent control analysis or stepped-wedge design is required before hospital-wide extension.
debate_rounds.defender: Round 1: Full concession. Leadership's conclusion that the model is improving care is not supported by the current analysis.

points_resolved: Issue 3: lactate ordering cohort selection — critique_wins
all_issues_raised: Issue 2: three concurrent interventions not separable
Issue 3: model-influenced lactate ordering changes cohort

empirical_test.condition: Prior-year same-season comparison; concurrent control ED with antibiotic protocol but without model; lactate ordering rate analysis
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Prior-year November-December shows similar mortality; control EDs with antibiotic protocol show comparable improvement without model
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Model-active EDs show lower mortality than concurrent same-protocol controls in same season
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Some model contribution but confounds dominate the estimated effect size
